text.title: Lookalike and Retargeting Audience Design Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of Lookalike and Retargeting Audience Design MUST enforce. Rationale + source per rule. Rules referenced by id from `content/06-decision-tree.xml`.

rule.statement: Seed audience MUST be >=1000 events with same outcome (purchase / qualified-lead). Smaller seeds produce noisy lookalikes.
rule.rationale: Meta and Google lookalike algorithms need >=1k anchors to model; smaller seeds yield random audiences disguised as targeted.

rule.statement: Every campaign MUST exclude existing customers, opt-outs, and recent-purchase 90-day cohort. Skipping exclusion is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Re-targeting existing customers wastes 15-40% of spend; exclusion is the highest-ROI move in audience design.

rule.statement: Audience overlap (campaigns serving same user) MUST be audited monthly. >30% overlap requires consolidation.
rule.rationale: Self-competition inflates CPM and dilutes attribution; monthly audit catches drift before it eats budget.

rule.statement: Frequency MUST be capped at 8 impressions/week per user. Above 8, creative fatigue dominates and CTR collapses.

rationale.source: Meta frequency studies + Google display cohort data

rule.rationale: Empirical curves show CTR collapse > 8 impressions; capping preserves account-level efficiency.

rule.statement: Lookalike degree MUST be 1-3% (Meta), top-5% similar (Google), top-10% matched (LinkedIn). Looser degrees collapse precision.

rationale.source: Platform best-practice docs + cross-platform benchmark Q1 2026

rule.rationale: Above the band the algorithm slips into broad-targeting; precision gains vanish.

rule.statement: If applicability preconditions are unmet (see AGENTS.md Skip If), the methodology MUST be skipped and a one-line skip-reason logged.
rule.rationale: A named skip terminal keeps decision-tree leaves resolvable and prevents silent misuse on misfit inputs.
